  • Tutorials
    • Transforming Variables
    • Making Comparisons
    • t-tests and ANOVAs
    • Chi-Square
    • Subsetting Data: Variable Types
    • Correlations
    • OLS: Linear Regressions
    • Logit: Logistic Regressions

    • Spain Replication data
    • Test Scores data

Multivariate Regression (Assumptions of OLS)

Author

Colin Hannigan

Agenda:

  1. Install Packages and Libraries
  2. Remember lm() In R
  3. Remember regC() In R
  4. Regressing Multiple IVs
  5. Checking Assumptions of OLS
    1. Linearity
    2. Representative Sampling
    3. No Multicollinearity
    4. No Heteroskedasticity
    5. Independence (No Autocorrelation)
    6. Normally Distributed Errors Around 0
  6. Application: Multivariate Linear Regression
    1. Re-scaling IVs
    2. Adding Control Variables
    3. Changing the Base Category (Re-leveling)
  7. Advanced Material

1. Install Packages and Libraries:

New packages to install:

No new packages to install this time!

Load libraries!

library(RCPA3)

2. Remember lm() In R

Recall that lm() is one of the functions for producing a linear model (i.e., line of best fit). The lm() function comes with base R, and we sometimes use it for running OLS regressions because it allows us to create a model object for future evaluation and manipulation.

Just like last time, we‚Äôre gonna regress states$covid.cases.per1000 against states$covid.deaths.per1000 to see whether they are significantly related (i.e., whether Covid cases are a significant predictor of Covid deaths). We‚Äôll store it in an object called lm1 to store that info for later and view the model summary below.

lm1 <- lm(states$covid.deaths.per1000 ~ states$covid.cases.per1000)
summary(lm1)

Call:
lm(formula = states$covid.deaths.per1000 ~ states$covid.cases.per1000)

Residuals:
     Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
-1.36143 -0.21312 -0.03666  0.20825  1.00972 

Coefficients:
                            Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)                0.0001036  0.2924364   0.000        1    
states$covid.cases.per1000 0.0166105  0.0028538   5.821 4.71e-07 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 0.4726 on 48 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.4138,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.4016 
F-statistic: 33.88 on 1 and 48 DF,  p-value: 4.707e-07

3. Remember regC() in R

Other times, we‚Äôll use the regC() function from the RCPA3 package to run the same linear regression test because that function comes with simplified options for evaluating the appropriateness of our model. The basic syntax is more or less the same between these two functions.

regC(states$covid.deaths.per1000 ~ states$covid.cases.per1000)
===========================================================================
                        Linear Regression Analysis
===========================================================================


Table: Linear Regression Coefficients

                              Estimate   Std. Error   t value   Pr(>|t|)
---------------------------  ---------  -----------  --------  ---------
(Intercept)                          0        0.292         0          1
states$covid.cases.per1000       0.017        0.003     5.821      <.001


Table: Regression Residuals

   Min.   1st Qu.   Median   3rd Qu.    Max.
-------  --------  -------  --------  ------
 -1.361    -0.213   -0.037     0.208   1.010


Additional Information:

Residual standard error: 0.4726 on 48 degrees of freedom
N: 50 (0 observations deleted due to missingness)
Multiple R-squared:  0.4138,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.4016 
F-statistic: 33.88 on 1 and 48 DF,  p-value: 4.707e-07
Dependent variable: states$covid.deaths.per1000

4. Regressing Multiple IVs

So far, we‚Äôve only looked at bivariate regressions, but regressions can handle multiple variables at once, which is crucial for accounting for interactions, additive explanations, control variables, etc. to produce stronger, more accurate models.

So, let‚Äôs add more covariates to our original model and try again.

lm2 <- lm(formula = states$covid.deaths.per1000 ~ states$covid.cases.per1000 + states$obesity.percent + states$gini.2016 + states$prcapinc + states$urban)
summary(lm2)

Call:
lm(formula = states$covid.deaths.per1000 ~ states$covid.cases.per1000 + 
    states$obesity.percent + states$gini.2016 + states$prcapinc + 
    states$urban)

Residuals:
     Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
-0.63263 -0.22202 -0.04042  0.22996  0.59644 

Coefficients:
                             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)                -9.433e+00  1.241e+00  -7.600 1.53e-09 ***
states$covid.cases.per1000  1.365e-02  2.109e-03   6.473 6.80e-08 ***
states$obesity.percent      2.660e-02  1.689e-02   1.575   0.1225    
states$gini.2016            1.704e+01  2.711e+00   6.286 1.28e-07 ***
states$prcapinc             3.017e-05  1.327e-05   2.274   0.0279 *  
states$urban                9.547e-04  4.132e-03   0.231   0.8183    
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 0.3199 on 44 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.7538,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.7258 
F-statistic: 26.94 on 5 and 44 DF,  p-value: 2.295e-12
regC(formula = states$covid.deaths.per1000 ~ states$covid.cases.per1000 + states$obesity.percent + states$gini.2016 + states$prcapinc + states$urban)
===========================================================================
                        Linear Regression Analysis
===========================================================================


Table: Linear Regression Coefficients

                              Estimate   Std. Error   t value   Pr(>|t|)
---------------------------  ---------  -----------  --------  ---------
(Intercept)                     -9.433        1.241      -7.6      <.001
states$covid.cases.per1000       0.014        0.002     6.473      <.001
states$obesity.percent           0.027        0.017     1.575      0.122
states$gini.2016                17.041        2.711     6.286      <.001
states$prcapinc                      0            0     2.274      0.028
states$urban                     0.001        0.004     0.231      0.818


Table: Regression Residuals

   Min.   1st Qu.   Median   3rd Qu.    Max.
-------  --------  -------  --------  ------
 -0.633    -0.222   -0.040     0.230   0.596


Additional Information:

Residual standard error: 0.3199 on 44 degrees of freedom
N: 50 (0 observations deleted due to missingness)
Multiple R-squared:  0.7538,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.7258 
F-statistic: 26.94 on 5 and 44 DF,  p-value: 2.295e-12
Dependent variable: states$covid.deaths.per1000

Run the models and interpret the results technically and substantively!

5. Checking Assumptions of OLS

As we‚Äôve discussed, Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) regression requires a lot of assumptions to be true in order for us to make strong and appropriate inferences from the output.When these assumptions are violated, we have to make adjustments to the variables we include, to the measurement of those variables, or to the model itself. Sometimes we find that OLS isn‚Äôt appropriate at all.

Linearity

To establish that the model meets the assumption of linearity, we can do a basic visual inspection of the relationship between each IV and the DV to make sure each distribution is more or less linear, but that only goes so far (and it‚Äôs often tedious‚Ä¶).

plot(y = states$covid.deaths.per1000, x = states$covid.cases.per1000)
# plot(y = states$covid.deaths.per1000, x = states$obesity.percent)
# plot(y = states$covid.deaths.per1000, x = states$gini.2016)
# plot(y = states$covid.deaths.per1000, x = states$prcapinc)
# plot(y = states$covid.deaths.per1000, x = states$urban)

Relying on only a visual inspection of the scatterplots can also be misleading. So, to be more certain, we can run a statistical test to determine whether the assumption is violated. This is where regC() comes in handy: we just have to add the linear.test = T option at the end:

regC(formula = states$covid.deaths.per1000 ~ states$covid.cases.per1000 + states$obesity.percent + states$gini.2016 + states$prcapinc + states$urban, linear.test = T)
===========================================================================
                        Linear Regression Analysis
===========================================================================


Table: Linear Regression Coefficients

                              Estimate   Std. Error   t value   Pr(>|t|)
---------------------------  ---------  -----------  --------  ---------
(Intercept)                     -9.433        1.241      -7.6      <.001
states$covid.cases.per1000       0.014        0.002     6.473      <.001
states$obesity.percent           0.027        0.017     1.575      0.122
states$gini.2016                17.041        2.711     6.286      <.001
states$prcapinc                      0            0     2.274      0.028
states$urban                     0.001        0.004     0.231      0.818


Table: Regression Residuals

   Min.   1st Qu.   Median   3rd Qu.    Max.
-------  --------  -------  --------  ------
 -0.633    -0.222   -0.040     0.230   0.596


Additional Information:

Residual standard error: 0.3199 on 44 degrees of freedom
N: 50 (0 observations deleted due to missingness)
Multiple R-squared:  0.7538,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.7258 
F-statistic: 26.94 on 5 and 44 DF,  p-value: 2.295e-12
Dependent variable: states$covid.deaths.per1000

---------------------------------------------------------------------------
                             Test of Linearity
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
Null Hypothesis: The relationship in regression model is linear. 


    Harvey-Collier test

data:  reg.model
HC = 0.43553, df = 43, p-value = 0.6654

This is the Harvey-Collier test, for which the null hypothesis is that the model is linear.

  • significant p-value: we have problems
  • non significant p-value: we fail to reject the null and we‚Äôre all good!

Representative Sampling

We can‚Äôt really test for representative sampling statistically. Instead, the key is to read the documentation/codebook for the dataset to ensure that the creators provide a full population of cases or a random, stratified, representative sample!

No Multicollinearity (or Perfect Collinearity)

A quick, low-tech way to try to prevent multicollinearity problems in a linear regression model is to check the correlations between each pair of IVs.

correlateC(c(states$covid.cases.per1000, states$obesity.percent, states$gini.2016, states$prcapinc, states$urban))
===========================================================================
                           Correlation Analysis
===========================================================================


Table: Correlation among states$covid.cases.per1000, states$obesity.percent, states$gini.2016, states$prcapinc, states$urban

                              states$covid.cases.per1000   states$obesity.percent   states$gini.2016   states$prcapinc   states$urban
---------------------------  ---------------------------  -----------------------  -----------------  ----------------  -------------
states$covid.cases.per1000                         1.000                    0.279              0.206            -0.180          0.080
states$obesity.percent                             0.279                    1.000              0.138            -0.569         -0.495
states$gini.2016                                   0.206                    0.138              1.000             0.088          0.254
states$prcapinc                                   -0.180                   -0.569              0.088             1.000          0.495
states$urban                                       0.080                   -0.495              0.254             0.495          1.000

While that can help us identify any glaring problems right away, this method isn‚Äôt very systematic; it doesn‚Äôt provide a complete picture of how all of the IVs are associated with one another. So, we go back to regC() and add the vif = T option:

regC(formula = states$covid.deaths.per1000 ~ states$covid.cases.per1000 + states$obesity.percent + states$gini.2016 + states$prcapinc + states$urban, vif = T)
===========================================================================
                        Linear Regression Analysis
===========================================================================


Table: Linear Regression Coefficients

                              Estimate   Std. Error   t value   Pr(>|t|)
---------------------------  ---------  -----------  --------  ---------
(Intercept)                     -9.433        1.241      -7.6      <.001
states$covid.cases.per1000       0.014        0.002     6.473      <.001
states$obesity.percent           0.027        0.017     1.575      0.122
states$gini.2016                17.041        2.711     6.286      <.001
states$prcapinc                      0            0     2.274      0.028
states$urban                     0.001        0.004     0.231      0.818


Table: Regression Residuals

   Min.   1st Qu.   Median   3rd Qu.    Max.
-------  --------  -------  --------  ------
 -0.633    -0.222   -0.040     0.230   0.596


Additional Information:

Residual standard error: 0.3199 on 44 degrees of freedom
N: 50 (0 observations deleted due to missingness)
Multiple R-squared:  0.7538,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.7258 
F-statistic: 26.94 on 5 and 44 DF,  p-value: 2.295e-12
Dependent variable: states$covid.deaths.per1000

---------------------------------------------------------------------------
                        Variance Inflation Factors
---------------------------------------------------------------------------


Table: Variance Inflation Factors

                                VIF
---------------------------  ------
states$covid.cases.per1000    1.191
states$obesity.percent        1.942
states$gini.2016              1.211
states$prcapinc               1.668
states$urban                  1.735

The Variance Inflation Factor (vif) calculates a numeric score reflecting how much the variance of each coefficient is inflated in the model. In other words, it helps us figure out how big of a multicollinearity problem we have.

Usually, VIF values of over 10 indicate a problem with multicollinearity, and we can remove the offending variable(s) and re-run the model without it (always reporting in the paper that the variable was removed and why).

In our example, we don‚Äôt have any issues with multicollinearity, which is a good sign!

No Heteroskedasticity

With regC(), we have a couple of options to look for heteroskedasticity problems. We can use the res.plots = T option to visually inspect the Residual vs. Fitted plots (the top left plot that appears with this option).

Part of the regression process involves predicting values of y (i.e., ‚Äúfitted‚Äù values), so we‚Äôre trying to ensure that the residuals (i.e., errors) aren‚Äôt changing too much based on the predictions of y. In other words, we‚Äôre hoping to see a straight line around 0.

regC(formula = states$covid.deaths.per1000 ~ states$covid.cases.per1000 + states$obesity.percent + states$gini.2016 + states$prcapinc + states$urban, res.plots = T)
===========================================================================
                        Linear Regression Analysis
===========================================================================


Table: Linear Regression Coefficients

                              Estimate   Std. Error   t value   Pr(>|t|)
---------------------------  ---------  -----------  --------  ---------
(Intercept)                     -9.433        1.241      -7.6      <.001
states$covid.cases.per1000       0.014        0.002     6.473      <.001
states$obesity.percent           0.027        0.017     1.575      0.122
states$gini.2016                17.041        2.711     6.286      <.001
states$prcapinc                      0            0     2.274      0.028
states$urban                     0.001        0.004     0.231      0.818


Table: Regression Residuals

   Min.   1st Qu.   Median   3rd Qu.    Max.
-------  --------  -------  --------  ------
 -0.633    -0.222   -0.040     0.230   0.596


Additional Information:

Residual standard error: 0.3199 on 44 degrees of freedom
N: 50 (0 observations deleted due to missingness)
Multiple R-squared:  0.7538,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.7258 
F-statistic: 26.94 on 5 and 44 DF,  p-value: 2.295e-12
Dependent variable: states$covid.deaths.per1000

But data is imperfect and there‚Äôs always uncertainty, so we‚Äôre not very likely to see a perfectly straight line. Some little squiggles are fine, but understanding the difference takes practice and can totally depend on scale.

So, that‚Äôs where option 2 comes in: we can add the Non-Constant Variance test (ncv.test = T argument) to our regC() function to test for heteroskedasticity more systematically.

regC(formula = states$covid.deaths.per1000 ~ states$covid.cases.per1000 + states$obesity.percent + states$gini.2016 + states$prcapinc + states$urban, ncv.test = T)
===========================================================================
                        Linear Regression Analysis
===========================================================================


Table: Linear Regression Coefficients

                              Estimate   Std. Error   t value   Pr(>|t|)
---------------------------  ---------  -----------  --------  ---------
(Intercept)                     -9.433        1.241      -7.6      <.001
states$covid.cases.per1000       0.014        0.002     6.473      <.001
states$obesity.percent           0.027        0.017     1.575      0.122
states$gini.2016                17.041        2.711     6.286      <.001
states$prcapinc                      0            0     2.274      0.028
states$urban                     0.001        0.004     0.231      0.818


Table: Regression Residuals

   Min.   1st Qu.   Median   3rd Qu.    Max.
-------  --------  -------  --------  ------
 -0.633    -0.222   -0.040     0.230   0.596


Additional Information:

Residual standard error: 0.3199 on 44 degrees of freedom
N: 50 (0 observations deleted due to missingness)
Multiple R-squared:  0.7538,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.7258 
F-statistic: 26.94 on 5 and 44 DF,  p-value: 2.295e-12
Dependent variable: states$covid.deaths.per1000

---------------------------------------------------------------------------
                     Non-Constant Error Variance Test
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
Null hypothesis: Residuals have constant variance. 

    studentized Breusch-Pagan test

data:  reg.model
BP = 2.5465, df = 5, p-value = 0.7695

This is the Breusch-Pagan test, for which the null hypothesis is that the model residuals have constant variance (i.e., that they‚Äôre homoskedastic).

  • significant p-value: we have problems
  • non significant p-value: we fail to reject the null and we‚Äôre all good!

Independence (No Autocorrelation)

The regC() function doesn‚Äôt have a dedicated optional argument for testing the assumption of independence. But we can do a check by running a separate on our model. The dwtest() function comes from the lmtest package, but we won‚Äôt need to install that package because RCPA3 imports it already. Instead of loading the whole library, we‚Äôll just call the function and tell R where to find it: lmtest::dwtest().

This is a situation where it‚Äôs helpful to have an lm() model object stored. But even if we don‚Äôt have one, we can quickly create one, or just paste the model formula into the dwtest() function directly:

lmtest::dwtest(formula = states$covid.deaths.per1000 ~ states$covid.cases.per1000 + states$obesity.percent + states$gini.2016 + states$prcapinc)

    Durbin-Watson test

data:  states$covid.deaths.per1000 ~ states$covid.cases.per1000 + states$obesity.percent +     states$gini.2016 + states$prcapinc
DW = 2.08, p-value = 0.5838
alternative hypothesis: true autocorrelation is greater than 0

This is the Durbin-Watson test, for which the null hypothesis is that there is no autocorrelation (i.e., that the observations are independent).

  • significant p-value: we have problems.
  • non significant p-value: we fail to reject the null and we‚Äôre all good!

Normally Distributed Errors Around 0

Similarly to our tests for heteroskedasticity, we can test for normally distributed errors visually or statistically. The visual test once again involves including the res.plots = T argument in regC(), this time looking at the Normal Q-Q plots (the top right plot that appears with this option) to ensure a straight positive line along the diagonal.

regC(formula = states$covid.deaths.per1000 ~ states$covid.cases.per1000 + states$obesity.percent + states$gini.2016 + states$prcapinc, res.plots = T)
===========================================================================
                        Linear Regression Analysis
===========================================================================


Table: Linear Regression Coefficients

                              Estimate   Std. Error   t value   Pr(>|t|)
---------------------------  ---------  -----------  --------  ---------
(Intercept)                      -9.44        1.228    -7.689      <.001
states$covid.cases.per1000       0.014        0.002     6.792      <.001
states$obesity.percent           0.025        0.015     1.641      0.108
states$gini.2016                17.229        2.559     6.733      <.001
states$prcapinc                      0            0     2.448      0.018


Table: Regression Residuals

   Min.   1st Qu.   Median   3rd Qu.    Max.
-------  --------  -------  --------  ------
 -0.635    -0.220   -0.032     0.237   0.586


Additional Information:

Residual standard error: 0.3165 on 45 degrees of freedom
N: 50 (0 observations deleted due to missingness)
Multiple R-squared:  0.7535,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.7316 
F-statistic: 34.38 on 4 and 45 DF,  p-value: 3.727e-13
Dependent variable: states$covid.deaths.per1000

But because we run into similar problems of ambiguity, we can rely on the norm.test = T argument to our regC() function instead.

regC(formula = states$covid.deaths.per1000 ~ states$covid.cases.per1000 + states$obesity.percent + states$gini.2016 + states$prcapinc, norm.test = T)
===========================================================================
                        Linear Regression Analysis
===========================================================================


Table: Linear Regression Coefficients

                              Estimate   Std. Error   t value   Pr(>|t|)
---------------------------  ---------  -----------  --------  ---------
(Intercept)                      -9.44        1.228    -7.689      <.001
states$covid.cases.per1000       0.014        0.002     6.792      <.001
states$obesity.percent           0.025        0.015     1.641      0.108
states$gini.2016                17.229        2.559     6.733      <.001
states$prcapinc                      0            0     2.448      0.018


Table: Regression Residuals

   Min.   1st Qu.   Median   3rd Qu.    Max.
-------  --------  -------  --------  ------
 -0.635    -0.220   -0.032     0.237   0.586


Additional Information:

Residual standard error: 0.3165 on 45 degrees of freedom
N: 50 (0 observations deleted due to missingness)
Multiple R-squared:  0.7535,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.7316 
F-statistic: 34.38 on 4 and 45 DF,  p-value: 3.727e-13
Dependent variable: states$covid.deaths.per1000

---------------------------------------------------------------------------
                    Test of the Normality of Residuals
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
Null Hypothesis: Residuals follow normal distribution. 

        Jarque-Bera Test 

JB test stat.       p-value 
        1.524         0.467

This is the Jarque-Bera test, for which the null hypothesis is that the residuals are normally distributed.

  • significant p-value: we have problems.
  • non significant p-value: we fail to reject the null and we‚Äôre all good!

6. Application: Multivariate Linear Regression

Now that we know that our original model doesn‚Äôt violate the assumptions of OLS, let‚Äôs reproduce the simplified version of it using regC() for comparison:

regC(formula = states$covid.deaths.per1000 ~ states$covid.cases.per1000 + states$obesity.percent + states$gini.2016 + states$prcapinc + states$urban)
===========================================================================
                        Linear Regression Analysis
===========================================================================


Table: Linear Regression Coefficients

                              Estimate   Std. Error   t value   Pr(>|t|)
---------------------------  ---------  -----------  --------  ---------
(Intercept)                     -9.433        1.241      -7.6      <.001
states$covid.cases.per1000       0.014        0.002     6.473      <.001
states$obesity.percent           0.027        0.017     1.575      0.122
states$gini.2016                17.041        2.711     6.286      <.001
states$prcapinc                      0            0     2.274      0.028
states$urban                     0.001        0.004     0.231      0.818


Table: Regression Residuals

   Min.   1st Qu.   Median   3rd Qu.    Max.
-------  --------  -------  --------  ------
 -0.633    -0.222   -0.040     0.230   0.596


Additional Information:

Residual standard error: 0.3199 on 44 degrees of freedom
N: 50 (0 observations deleted due to missingness)
Multiple R-squared:  0.7538,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.7258 
F-statistic: 26.94 on 5 and 44 DF,  p-value: 2.295e-12
Dependent variable: states$covid.deaths.per1000

Re-scaling IVs

The elephant in the room is that we have a significant partial effect for states$prcapinc but both the coefficient estimate and standard error are 0, which doesn‚Äôt make sense.

But if we were to run describeC() on states$prcapinc, we‚Äôd see that even the minimum of per capita income by state is over $20,000! So, when we try to interpret what the change in Covid deaths per thousand people is for every one-unit change in per capita income, we‚Äôre actually looking at how much $1 changes Covid deaths per thousand people. The effect of a single dollar is so tiny that it rounds to 0.

One solution to this problem is to re-scale the variable for clearer interpretation. For example, we could measure per capita income in thousands of dollars. That way, a one-unit increase in the variable represents an increase of $1,000 dollars. First we‚Äôll create a new, re-scaled variable from the original, then we‚Äôll plug it into the function and run the model again:

prcapinc.thousands <- states$prcapinc/1000

regC(formula = states$covid.deaths.per1000 ~ states$covid.cases.per1000 + states$obesity.percent + states$gini.2016 + prcapinc.thousands + states$urban)
===========================================================================
                        Linear Regression Analysis
===========================================================================


Table: Linear Regression Coefficients

                              Estimate   Std. Error   t value   Pr(>|t|)
---------------------------  ---------  -----------  --------  ---------
(Intercept)                     -9.433        1.241      -7.6      <.001
states$covid.cases.per1000       0.014        0.002     6.473      <.001
states$obesity.percent           0.027        0.017     1.575      0.122
states$gini.2016                17.041        2.711     6.286      <.001
prcapinc.thousands                0.03        0.013     2.274      0.028
states$urban                     0.001        0.004     0.231      0.818


Table: Regression Residuals

   Min.   1st Qu.   Median   3rd Qu.    Max.
-------  --------  -------  --------  ------
 -0.633    -0.222   -0.040     0.230   0.596


Additional Information:

Residual standard error: 0.3199 on 44 degrees of freedom
N: 50 (0 observations deleted due to missingness)
Multiple R-squared:  0.7538,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.7258 
F-statistic: 26.94 on 5 and 44 DF,  p-value: 2.295e-12
Dependent variable: states$covid.deaths.per1000

Everything else looks basically the same, right? But now we can actually interpret the partial effect of per capita income: for every one-unit increase in per capita income (in thousands), there is a 0.03-unit increase in Covid deaths per 1000 in a given state.

Adding Control Variables

Now, let‚Äôs consider adding states$region to the model as a control variable to help explain Covid deaths per 1000 people.

Why might region be a good control variable? Consider the causal mechanisms.

First, we‚Äôll take a look at it to see what kind of variable it is:

attributes(states$region)
$levels
[1] "Northeast" "Midwest"   "South"     "West"     

$class
[1] "factor"

$label
[1] "Census region"

It‚Äôs a factor variable with 4 categories: Northeast, Midwest, South, and West.

Let‚Äôs see what R does with a factor variable when we include it in a regression:

regC(formula = states$covid.deaths.per1000 ~ states$covid.cases.per1000 + states$obesity.percent + states$gini.2016 + prcapinc.thousands + states$urban + states$region)
===========================================================================
                        Linear Regression Analysis
===========================================================================


Table: Linear Regression Coefficients

                              Estimate   Std. Error   t value   Pr(>|t|)
---------------------------  ---------  -----------  --------  ---------
(Intercept)                     -8.261        1.614     -5.12      <.001
states$covid.cases.per1000       0.013        0.002     6.133      <.001
states$obesity.percent           0.037        0.021     1.774      0.083
states$gini.2016                15.357        3.122     4.918      <.001
prcapinc.thousands               0.007        0.017     0.401      0.691
states$urban                     0.006        0.005     1.371      0.178
states$regionMidwest            -0.302        0.183    -1.647      0.107
states$regionSouth              -0.429         0.18    -2.382      0.022
states$regionWest               -0.411        0.191    -2.158      0.037


Table: Regression Residuals

   Min.   1st Qu.   Median   3rd Qu.    Max.
-------  --------  -------  --------  ------
 -0.501    -0.227   -0.034     0.248   0.624


Additional Information:

Residual standard error: 0.3067 on 41 degrees of freedom
N: 50 (0 observations deleted due to missingness)
Multiple R-squared:  0.7891,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.748 
F-statistic: 19.18 on 8 and 41 DF,  p-value: 1.345e-11
Dependent variable: states$covid.deaths.per1000

Notice how it automatically breaks it out into dummy variables for us? That‚Äôs super helpful for interpretation.

But where‚Äôs our other category, ‚ÄúNortheast‚Äù??

It‚Äôs the ‚Äúreference‚Äù or ‚Äúbase‚Äù category! It‚Äôs excluded to avoid an error of perfect collinearity. Including all of the dummy variables would lead to a math error because the sum of all of the dummies would always equal 1 (they‚Äôre exhaustive and they don‚Äôt overlap).

But what if we‚Äôre particularly interested in the behavior of the Northeast region in particular?

Changing the Base Category (Re-leveling)

The reference category defaults to the ‚Äúfirst‚Äù one listed in the variable levels (which we can see at the top of the freqC() table, for example). But we can always change the reference category by re-leveling the variable, which tells regC() which category to exclude instead.

We can do this a couple of ways using the relevel() function. Either by including it directly in the model formula for a quick check‚Ä¶

# We can change the base category in the model using relevel()...
regC(formula = states$covid.deaths.per1000 ~ states$covid.cases.per1000 + states$obesity.percent + states$gini.2016 + prcapinc.thousands + states$urban + relevel(states$region, "Midwest"))
===========================================================================
                        Linear Regression Analysis
===========================================================================


Table: Linear Regression Coefficients

                                              Estimate   Std. Error   t value   Pr(>|t|)
-------------------------------------------  ---------  -----------  --------  ---------
(Intercept)                                     -8.564        1.571     -5.45      <.001
states$covid.cases.per1000                       0.013        0.002     6.133      <.001
states$obesity.percent                           0.037        0.021     1.774      0.083
states$gini.2016                                15.357        3.122     4.918      <.001
prcapinc.thousands                               0.007        0.017     0.401      0.691
states$urban                                     0.006        0.005     1.371      0.178
relevel(states$region, "Midwest")Northeast       0.302        0.183     1.647      0.107
relevel(states$region, "Midwest")South          -0.127        0.135    -0.942      0.352
relevel(states$region, "Midwest")West           -0.109        0.167    -0.651      0.518


Table: Regression Residuals

   Min.   1st Qu.   Median   3rd Qu.    Max.
-------  --------  -------  --------  ------
 -0.501    -0.227   -0.034     0.248   0.624


Additional Information:

Residual standard error: 0.3067 on 41 degrees of freedom
N: 50 (0 observations deleted due to missingness)
Multiple R-squared:  0.7891,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.748 
F-statistic: 19.18 on 8 and 41 DF,  p-value: 1.345e-11
Dependent variable: states$covid.deaths.per1000

‚Ä¶or by creating a new variable that we re-level in advance. Because of how regC() labels variable categories when dummying them out, I like to name the new variable with an underscore or something at the end to help keep the model output tidy when we re-run the model with the new variable:

region.New_ <- relevel(states$region, "Midwest")

regC(formula = states$covid.deaths.per1000 ~ states$covid.cases.per1000 + states$obesity.percent + states$gini.2016 + prcapinc.thousands + states$urban + region.New_)
===========================================================================
                        Linear Regression Analysis
===========================================================================


Table: Linear Regression Coefficients

                              Estimate   Std. Error   t value   Pr(>|t|)
---------------------------  ---------  -----------  --------  ---------
(Intercept)                     -8.564        1.571     -5.45      <.001
states$covid.cases.per1000       0.013        0.002     6.133      <.001
states$obesity.percent           0.037        0.021     1.774      0.083
states$gini.2016                15.357        3.122     4.918      <.001
prcapinc.thousands               0.007        0.017     0.401      0.691
states$urban                     0.006        0.005     1.371      0.178
region.New_Northeast             0.302        0.183     1.647      0.107
region.New_South                -0.127        0.135    -0.942      0.352
region.New_West                 -0.109        0.167    -0.651      0.518


Table: Regression Residuals

   Min.   1st Qu.   Median   3rd Qu.    Max.
-------  --------  -------  --------  ------
 -0.501    -0.227   -0.034     0.248   0.624


Additional Information:

Residual standard error: 0.3067 on 41 degrees of freedom
N: 50 (0 observations deleted due to missingness)
Multiple R-squared:  0.7891,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.748 
F-statistic: 19.18 on 8 and 41 DF,  p-value: 1.345e-11
Dependent variable: states$covid.deaths.per1000

Notice how some of our other variables are no longer significant after we change the reference category?

That‚Äôs normal. It just means that controlling for region is picking up some of the patterns we‚Äôd previously attributed to other variables.

But if that‚Äôs the case, we should probably double check to ensure there‚Äôs still no issue with multicollinearity. Let‚Äôs add the vif = T argument again:

regC(formula = states$covid.deaths.per1000 ~ states$covid.cases.per1000 + states$obesity.percent + states$gini.2016 + prcapinc.thousands + states$urban + states$region, vif = T)
===========================================================================
                        Linear Regression Analysis
===========================================================================


Table: Linear Regression Coefficients

                              Estimate   Std. Error   t value   Pr(>|t|)
---------------------------  ---------  -----------  --------  ---------
(Intercept)                     -8.261        1.614     -5.12      <.001
states$covid.cases.per1000       0.013        0.002     6.133      <.001
states$obesity.percent           0.037        0.021     1.774      0.083
states$gini.2016                15.357        3.122     4.918      <.001
prcapinc.thousands               0.007        0.017     0.401      0.691
states$urban                     0.006        0.005     1.371      0.178
states$regionMidwest            -0.302        0.183    -1.647      0.107
states$regionSouth              -0.429         0.18    -2.382      0.022
states$regionWest               -0.411        0.191    -2.158      0.037


Table: Regression Residuals

   Min.   1st Qu.   Median   3rd Qu.    Max.
-------  --------  -------  --------  ------
 -0.501    -0.227   -0.034     0.248   0.624


Additional Information:

Residual standard error: 0.3067 on 41 degrees of freedom
N: 50 (0 observations deleted due to missingness)
Multiple R-squared:  0.7891,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.748 
F-statistic: 19.18 on 8 and 41 DF,  p-value: 1.345e-11
Dependent variable: states$covid.deaths.per1000

---------------------------------------------------------------------------
                        Variance Inflation Factors
---------------------------------------------------------------------------


Table: Variance Inflation Factors

                                VIF      NA      NA
---------------------------  ------  ------  ------
states$covid.cases.per1000    1.358   1.000   1.165
states$obesity.percent        3.307   1.000   1.819
states$gini.2016              1.748   1.000   1.322
prcapinc.thousands            2.867   1.000   1.693
states$urban                  2.382   1.000   1.543
states$region                 6.151   3.000   1.354

Nothing above 10, so we‚Äôre good!

7. Advanced Material
